occurrenceremarks: Native mustard plant growing in dry, gravelly, seasonal river bed in the area that was heavily burned in August 2020 River Fire. This plant is a fire-follower. California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus) Native, annual plant in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. A fire follower. Plant grows 20-100cm (up to 3.25’) tall. Leaves are long, generally simple, and alternate on stem. Sap is pungent and watery. It has white to creamy-white flowers. Peak bloom time: March-June. Cylindrical fruits can be erect to reflexed, straight to out curved. (This is unlike Tower Mustard’s fruits which are erect and appressed to stem). Peak bloom time: April. Monterey County Wildflowers: a Field Guide, Yeager and Mitchell, 2016, p. 204. Plants of Monterey County: an Illustrated Field Key, 2nd edition, Matthews and Mitchell, 2015, pp. 108-109. Jepson eFlora https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=18404 Flora of Fort Ord: Monterey County, California, David Styer, 2019, p. 75.
